brands.name: Daniel Smith
brands.tagline: For when polite paint won't cut it
story: Daniel Smith is what happens when a paint company stops pretending subtlety is a virtue. Their Primatek line literally grinds rocks — lapis, amethyst, iron ore — and asks your paper to deal with it.
The granulation is not a bug. It's the whole personality. Skies turn moody, stones look ancient, and boring grey clouds suddenly have opinions.
Quinacridones? Obnoxiously bright in the best way. A little staining, so maybe don't audition them on your wedding-invite practice sheet.
Fat tubes, reliable rewet, Seattle energy. If your palette needs one dramatic friend, this is them.

artists.name: Joseph Zbukvic
artists.note: Melbourne wizard of wet city light. Basically the DS poster child — atmosphere so thick you could lean on it.

works.title: The Piazzetta, Venice
works.caption: We can't host Zbukvic's copyrighted work — Turner's wet Venice is the public-domain cousin for that atmosphere.

artists.name: Alvaro Castagnet
artists.note: Gesture first, apology never. He made a generation believe bold washes are a lifestyle.

works.title: Muddy Alligators
works.caption: Castagnet's own paintings are still copyrighted — Sargent's bold wash swagger is the free demo cousin.

artists.name: Winslow Homer
artists.note: American no-nonsense values. DS granulating blues were born for this kind of blunt beauty.

works.title: The Blue Boat
works.caption: Three strokes and done. Homer would hate your 47-layer sky.

brands.name: Schmincke
brands.tagline: German engineering, but make it creamy
story: Schmincke Horadam is the overachiever who actually reads the syllabus. Finely milled, balanced, deeply un-dramatic in the best way.
Creamy in the palette, civilized on the paper, lifts like it remembers you have plans later.
Almost no grain when you rewet — which is why detail painters quietly marry this brand and never post about it on social media.
We keep a lot of Schmincke in the studio. My hot take: if you're fighting your paint, switch here and stop blaming your brush.

artists.name: Albrecht Dürer
artists.note: Painted a clump of grass in 1503 and humiliated every lazy sketcher since.

works.title: The Large Piece of Turf
works.caption: Botanical nerdery before it was cute. Schmincke vibes, honestly.

artists.name: Paul Klee
artists.note: Built tiny color worlds with patience instead of shouting. Very Horadam.

works.title: Small Swiss Landscape
works.caption: Whisper-level washes. No fireworks required.

artists.name: Emil Nolde
artists.note: Florals so intense they should come with a warning label. Needs pigments that don't panic when layered.

works.title: Summer Evening
works.caption: Florals and skies that refuse to whisper. Needs pigments that don't panic when layered.

brands.name: Winsor & Newton
brands.tagline: Your art-school ex who still texts
story: Winsor & Newton is the watercolor everyone met first — usually in a dented tin with someone's name scratched inside.
Broad range, stocked on every continent, tuned for British manners: transparent washes, controlled blooms, white paper doing honest work.
Yes they reformulated. No the color names didn't change. Thank you, W&N, for not gaslighting us.
Not sexy. Extremely reliable. Like good socks. You need those.

artists.name: J.M.W. Turner
artists.note: Invented atmospheric drama and possibly fog itself.

works.title: The Piazzetta, Venice
works.caption: Venice dissolving into light. Rude. Gorgeous.

artists.name: John Singer Sargent
artists.note: Portrait royalty who discovered watercolor and immediately had more fun than all of us.

works.title: Muddy Alligators
works.caption: Florida swamp chic. Leave the white paper alone like it owes you money.

artists.name: Winslow Homer
artists.note: Would like your delicate fussing. Would not respect it.

works.title: The Blue Boat
works.caption: Big blue. White paper. Argument over.

brands.name: Sennelier
story: Sennelier made these for Impressionists loitering in Paris with easels and opinions. Honey in the binder — paint stays moist, silky, and a little pleased with itself.
Washes glow like someone left the afternoon inside the pigment.
Warm Mediterranean palette: ochres, violets, rose madders. Skin tones, old walls, sun-drunk grass.
If you like wet, sliding color and romantic excuses to paint outdoors, Sennelier is basically enabling you.

artists.name: Paul Cézanne
artists.note: Turned apples into geometry and made it look inevitable.

works.title: Montagne Sainte-Victoire
works.caption: Stack transparent patches until a mountain becomes solid. Magic, but stubborn.

artists.name: Pierre Bonnard
artists.note: Domestic light, outrageous color. Honey-tube energy before honey tubes were a brand strategy.

works.title: Norman Landscape
works.caption: Color that glows from inside the paper — Bonnard never met a dull green he couldn't seduce.

artists.name: Henri Matisse
artists.note: Cut the politeness. Color as appetite — Matisse would empty a Sennelier drawer without blinking.

works.title: Woman with a Hat
works.caption: Fauvism in a hat. When 'too much color' is exactly the right amount.

brands.name: M. Graham
story: M. Graham puts Pacific Northwest honey in the binder and suddenly your paint stops ghosting you mid-session.
Slippery, luminous, weirdly friendly — like the tube is on your side.
High pigment load: tiny squeeze, big puddle. Great if you mix lakes, not puddles.
Smaller catalog than the giants, but curated like a good playlist. No filler tracks.

artists.name: Charles Reid
artists.note: Loose figures, clean color, no overworking — honey paints for painters who leave the white paper alone.

works.title: Autumn Foliage with Two Youths Fishing
works.caption: Reid's demos are still under copyright — Homer's outdoor loose watercolor is the free cousin.

artists.name: Andrew Wyeth
artists.note: Quiet American hush — values first, drama second. Honey keeps the wash patient.

works.title: Adirondack Lake
works.caption: Wyeth's estate is still protected — Homer's quiet lake stands in as the public-domain mood board.

artists.name: John Singer Sargent
artists.note: Travel watercolor mode: highlights bright, effort invisible.

works.title: Muddy Alligators
works.caption: Sun through transparent shadow. Brag quietly.

brands.name: MaimeriBlu
story: MaimeriBlu is Italy's old color house showing up with sunglasses and clean cobalts.
Gum arabic only — no honey — so washes stay crisp, like someone opened a window.
Earths honest, secondaries well-behaved, mixes that don't devolve into brown soup by accident.
Full pans are generous and rewet evenly. For orderly, glassy layers and painters who like their chaos planned.

artists.name: Giorgio Morandi
artists.note: Quiet bottles, quieter genius. Italian still-life patience — MaimeriBlu's clean earths were born for this hush.

works.title: Still-Life with Jar of Olives
works.caption: Morandi's estate is still protected — Chardin's quiet jars are the free still-life cousin.

artists.name: Canaletto
artists.note: Venice with backbone — architecture, light, and water that behaves (mostly).

works.title: The Grand Canal from Palazzo Flangini
works.caption: Venice drawn with a ruler and a love letter. Clear water, clear structure.

artists.name: Giovanni Boldini
artists.note: Italian swagger in a brushstroke. Fashionable, fast, a little dangerous.

works.title: An Elegant Lady
works.caption: Italian swagger in a brushstroke. Fashionable, fast, a little dangerous.

brands.name: White Nights
brands.tagline: Big pans, loud color, small price — go
story: White Nights is what Eastern Europe grew up squeezing — Nevskaya Palitra, serious pigment, not cafeteria chalk.
Pans are chunky and creamy; wake up fast. Beginners get saturation without sanding the paper.
Bright, a touch staining — commit like you meant it, not like you're apologizing.
Packaging won't woo you. Paint will. Starter set that doesn't insult your intelligence.

artists.name: Wassily Kandinsky
artists.note: Saw a circle, heard music, jumped. Russian boldness in one gesture.

works.title: Color Study, Squares with Concentric Circles
works.caption: Subject optional. Joy mandatory.

artists.name: Ilya Repin
artists.note: Story, weather, and human mess — Russian painting that never whispers.

works.title: What Freedom!
works.caption: Russian wind in the hair. Narrative watercolor energy with zero shy pigment.

artists.name: Nicholas Roerich
artists.note: Mythic color and horizon drama. Mountains of the mind, ships of the story.

works.title: Guests from Overseas
works.caption: Mythic color and horizon drama. Mountains of the mind, ships of the story.

brands.name: Rosa Gallery
story: Rosa Gallery shows up with Ukrainian full pans the size of small sandwiches and pigment that doesn't whisper.
Activates fast, hits bold, perfect when you need a big wash and a exit strategy.
Convenience mixes, loud secondaries — illustrators and urban sketchers love it because deadlines exist.
Subtle is not the brand brief. I grab Rosa when I want speed, saturation, and zero ceremony.

artists.name: Maria Prymachenko
artists.note: Ukrainian folk fire — beasts, flowers, and color that refuses to be polite.

works.title: The Ninth Wave
works.caption: Prymachenko's work is still protected — Aivazovsky's storm is the free Ukrainian-born drama cousin.

artists.name: Taras Shevchenko
artists.note: Poet with a paint box. Steppe light, clear line, no corporate landscape polish.

works.title: Landscape Study
works.caption: Poet with a paint box. Steppe light and honest paper.

artists.name: Arkhip Kuindzhi
artists.note: Impossible moonlight. Night that glows from the pigment out.

works.title: Moonlit Night on the Dnieper
works.caption: That impossible moonlight green-blue. Night that glows from the pigment out.

brands.name: Pinax
story: Pinax Extra is the half-pan that refuses mystery meat. Pigment code on the face, series letter, lightfast stars — homework already done.
The Extra line leans single-pigment: quin rose, pyrrol scarlet, ultramarine, potter's pink, iron oxides. Bright when you want noise, earthy when you want grit.
Russian branding, bold packaging, pans that activate like they mean business. Not every shade is subtle — some are cheerfully loud.
We added six from the studio photo: a red-pink corner, a mineral blue, and two granulating earths. Perfect bridge between student kits and full pro tins.

artists.name: Wassily Kandinsky
artists.note: Color as language. Pinax's pure singles feel like his squares — each pigment speaking for itself.

works.title: Color Study: Squares with Concentric Circles
works.caption: Single colors in conversation — the Extra-pan dream, public-domain edition.

artists.name: Nicholas Roerich
artists.note: Himalayan blues and stage-set color — mineral clarity, no apology.

works.title: Guests from Overseas
works.caption: Bold blocks of hue landing on the shore. Ultramarine energy before the tube was labeled PB29.

artists.name: Ilya Repin
artists.note: Russian realist fire — flesh, cloth, and sky that still teach mixing.

works.title: What Freedom!
works.caption: Wind, sea, and unfussy chroma. Paint that shows up for the story.

brands.name: Roman Szmal
brands.tagline: Polish pans that tell you what's inside
story: Roman Szmal Aquarius is the underrated professional line — honest pigment codes, fair price, no mystery goo.
Rewets like it remembers you. Classics plus a few fun extras, none of them 'sparkle chaos teal.'
Mixes stay clean — botanical painters, illustrators, anyone allergic to muddy surprises, hello.
Quiet brand, loud competence. Custom palette builders, take notes.

artists.name: Stanisław Wyspiański
artists.note: Art Nouveau tenderness with Polish backbone. Pastel-soft, design-strong.

works.title: Motherhood
works.caption: Art Nouveau tenderness with Polish backbone. Pastel-soft, design-strong.

artists.name: Józef Mehoffer
artists.note: Spring light and a red accent that steals the scene. Decorative without being fragile.

works.title: The Red Umbrella
works.caption: Spring light and a red accent that steals the scene. Decorative without being fragile.

artists.name: Albrecht Dürer
artists.note: Would still paint every blade with Aquarius pans. Respect the turf.

works.title: The Large Piece of Turf
works.caption: Transparency wins. Ego loses.
